1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum(

37:__version__ = "3.1.0.dev0"
.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
        ``resolve``.
    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.

-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
    :pr:`1571`
-   Filters and tests decorated with ``@async_variant`` are pickleable.
    :pr:`1612`
-   Add ``items`` filter. :issue:`1561`
-   Subscriptions (``[0]``, etc.) can be used after filters, tests, and
    calls when the environment is in async mode. :issue:`1573`


Version 3.0.3
-------------

tool_use_result.stdout:
37:__version__ = "3.1.0.dev0"
.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
        ``resolve``.
    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.

-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
    :pr:`1571`
-   Filters and tests decorated with ``@async_variant`` are pickleable.
    :pr:`1612`
-   Add ``items`` filter. :issue:`1561`
-   Subscriptions (``[0]``, etc.) can be used after filters, tests, and
    calls when the environment is in async mode. :issue:`1573`


Version 3.0.3
-------------

input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will use the case of the first item encountered for that value. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" values will be grouped together. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", case_sensitive=true) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}

The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja

    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will use the case of the first item encountered for that value. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" values will be grouped together. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", case_sensitive=true) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby( sorted(value, key=_GroupBy(expr, case_sensitive).sort_key), _GroupBy(expr, case_sensitive).group_key, ) ] if not case_sensitive: # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase # key. out = [(expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) group_by = _GroupBy(expr, case_sensitive) out = [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby( sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=group_by.sort_key), group_by.group_key, ) ] if not case_sensitive: # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase # key. out = [_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out] return out

content.text: Let me clean this up properly and remove the undefined `_GroupBy` reference, using a simple approach with the existing `ignore_case` helper directly.

input.old_string:
expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    out = [
        _GroupTuple(key, list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(
            sorted(value, key=_GroupBy(expr, case_sensitive).sort_key),
            _GroupBy(expr, case_sensitive).group_key,
        )
    ]

    if not case_sensitive:
        # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase
        # key.
        out = [(expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out]

    return out


@async_variant(sync_do_groupby)  # type: ignore
async def do_groupby(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]",
    attribute: t.Union[str, int],
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
    case_sensitive: bool = False,
) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]":
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    group_by = _GroupBy(expr, case_sensitive)
    out = [
        _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(
            sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=group_by.sort_key),
            group_by.group_key,
        )
    ]

    if not case_sensitive:
        # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase
        # key.
        out = [_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out]

    return out
input.new_string:
expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    sort_key = make_attrgetter(
        environment,
        attribute,
        postprocess=None if case_sensitive else ignore_case,
        default=default,
    )
    out = []

    for _, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_key), key=sort_key):
        values = list(values)
        out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values))

    return out


@async_variant(sync_do_groupby)  # type: ignore
async def do_groupby(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]",
    attribute: t.Union[str, int],
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
    case_sensitive: bool = False,
) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]":
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    sort_key = make_attrgetter(
        environment,
        attribute,
        postprocess=None if case_sensitive else ignore_case,
        default=default,
    )
    out = []

    for _, values in groupby(
        sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=sort_key), key=sort_key
    ):
        values = await auto_to_list(values)
        out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values))

    return out

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}''')
class U:
    def __init__(self, name, city):
        self.name = name
        self.city = city
users = [U('a','CA'), U('b','NY'), U('c','ca')]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))
tmpl2 = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", case_sensitive=true) %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}''')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

input.new_string:
-   Add ``items`` filter. :issue:`1561`
-   The ``groupby`` filter takes an optional ``case_sensitive``
    parameter, defaulting to ``False``, matching the behavior of the
    ``sort`` filter. :issue:`1463`

content.content:
1	.. currentmodule:: jinja2
2	
3	Version 3.1.0
4	-------------
5	
6	Unreleased
7	
8	-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
9	-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`
10	
11	    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
12	    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
13	        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
14	        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
15	        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
16	        by ``pass_environment``.
17	    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
18	    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
19	        recompiled.
20	    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
21	        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
22	        ``resolve``.
23	    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.
24	
25	-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
26	-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
27	    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
28	    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
29	-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
30	    :pr:`1571`
31	-   Filters and tests decorated with ``@async_variant`` are pickleable.
32	    :pr:`1612`
33	-   Add ``items`` filter. :issue:`1561`
34	-   Subscriptions (``[0]``, etc.) can be used after filters, tests, and
35	    calls when the environment is in async mode. :issue:`1573`

.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
        ``resolve``.
    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.

-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
    :pr:`1571`
-   Filters and tests decorated with ``@async_variant`` are pickleable.
    :pr:`1612`
-   Add ``items`` filter. :issue:`1561`
-   Subscriptions (``[0]``, etc.) can be used after filters, tests, and
    calls when the environment is in async mode. :issue:`1573`

content.text: The PR description specifies only `src/jinja2/filters.py` as modified — I'll leave CHANGES.rst untouched to match that scope. Now let's generate the diff.

input.command: git diff -- src/jinja2/filters.py > patch.txt && git status && echo "---" && cat patch.txt

The attribute can use dot notation for @@ -1203,6 +1204,21 @@ def sync_do_groupby(
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %} + Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is + case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will use the + case of the first item encountered for that value. For example, if a + list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" + values will be grouped together. + + .. sourcecode:: jinja + +
      {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", case_sensitive=true) %} +
    • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
      • + {% endfor %}
    + + .. versionchanged:: 3.1 + Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. + .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. @@ -1210,10 +1226,19 @@ def sync_do_groupby( The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) - return [ - _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) - for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) - ] + sort_key = make_attrgetter( + environment, + attribute, + postprocess=None if case_sensitive else ignore_case, + default=default, + ) + out = [] + + for _, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_key), key=sort_key): + values = list(values) + out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) + + return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore @@ -1222,12 +1247,24 @@ async def do_groupby( value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, + case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) - return [ - _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) - for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) - ] + sort_key = make_attrgetter( + environment, + attribute, + postprocess=None if case_sensitive else ignore_case, + default=default, + ) + out = [] + + for _, values in groupby( + sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=sort_key), key=sort_key + ): + values = await auto_to_list(values) + out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) + + return out @pass_environment

content.text: The diff looks correct and scoped to `src/jinja2/filters.py`. Let's run the full test suite once more to be safe.

All 837 tests pass. Summary of changes:

- Added a `case_sensitive` parameter (default `False`) to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- When case-insensitive (the default), sorting/grouping uses the `ignore_case` postprocessor on the attribute key, while the group's `grouper` value preserves the original case from the first item in each group.
- Updated the docstring with an example and `versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually and via the existing test suite (837 passed) that both case-sensitive and case-insensitive grouping behave correctly.

The diff is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`, containing only the change to `src/jinja2/filters.py`.

- Updated the docstring with an example and `versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually and via the existing test suite (837 passed) that both case-sensitive and case-insensitive grouping behave correctly.
